_meta.note: Generated by scripts/build-content-bundle.mjs — do not hand-edit; edit the /content source files.

guidelines.guideline: AHA/ASA 2021 Secondary Prevention; CHANCE / POINT / THALES / INSPIRES
guidelines.statement: In patients with minor non-cardioembolic ischemic stroke (NIHSS ≤3 or ≤5 with disabling deficit) or high-risk TIA (ABCD² ≥4 or DWI+), dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin plus clopidogrel for 21 days, or aspirin plus ticagrelor for 30 days) started within 24 h is recommended to reduce recurrent ischemic stroke at 90 days.
caveats: Bleeding risk increases with duration; do not extend DAPT beyond 21-30 days for this indication.
INSPIRES extended the window to 72 h in eligible mild stroke / high-risk TIA.
For CYP2C19 loss-of-function carriers, ticagrelor (CHANCE-2) is preferred over clopidogrel.

guidelines.guideline: AHA/ASA 2021 Secondary Prevention; OPTIMAS (2024); CATALYST IPDMA (2025); ELAN (2023); TIMING (2022)
guidelines.statement: In patients with AIS and atrial fibrillation, early DOAC initiation within ~4 days is reasonable across stroke severities (OPTIMAS: non-inferior across infarct sizes) and was superior to delayed start at 30 days in pooled individual-patient data (CATALYST IPDMA: OR 0.70, 95% CI 0.50-0.98, no sICH excess), excepting very severe stroke or extensive hemorrhagic transformation.
caveats: Reserve longer delay for very severe stroke or extensive hemorrhagic transformation; confirm absence of significant hemorrhagic transformation on follow-up imaging before starting in moderate-large stroke.
Early initiation (≤4 d) is non-inferior across severities (OPTIMAS) and superior at 30 d in pooled IPD (CATALYST); individualize for the highest-risk presentations.

guidelines.statement: In acute spontaneous ICH, continuous smooth, sustained BP control with timely treatment can be beneficial when BP lowering is indicated.
caveats: Smooth, sustained BP control and timely treatment are Class IIa process recommendations.
Do not present INTERACT3 as BP-only class evidence; it tested a bundle including BP, glucose, temperature, and anticoagulation reversal.
Avoid abrupt large drops in BP (>60 mmHg) which may worsen outcomes.
Do not pursue a numeric BP target in isolation from neurologic status and the full care bundle.

guidelines.statement: In mild-to-moderate acute spontaneous ICH with presenting SBP 150-220 mmHg, targeting SBP 140 mmHg and maintaining 130-150 mmHg when appropriate is safe and may be reasonable.
caveats: The SBP 140/range 130-150 target is Class IIb.
In patients with SBP >220 mmHg, the safety of aggressive lowering to 140 is less certain; use clinical judgment and continuous monitoring.
Use with the separate Class IIa process recommendation for smooth, sustained control and timely treatment.
Do not pursue a numeric BP target in isolation from neurologic status, hematoma severity, and neurosurgical planning.

guidelines.statement: Avoid acute SBP <130 mmHg in mild-to-moderate spontaneous ICH because it is potentially harmful.
caveats: Acute SBP <130 mmHg is Class III-harm.
This lower-bound harm guard is separate from the Class IIb SBP 140/range 130-150 target.

guidelines.statement: In factor Xa inhibitor-associated ICH within 15 hours of last dose, andexanet alfa is reasonable to achieve hemostatic efficacy when available; 4F-PCC is an alternative when andexanet is unavailable or contraindicated. Monitor for thrombotic complications.
caveats: ANNEXA-I demonstrated higher hemostatic efficacy with andexanet but more thrombotic complications vs usual care.
Andexanet alfa is not indicated for edoxaban; for edoxaban, 4F-PCC remains primary.
Reversal should not delay neurosurgical evaluation.

guidelines.statement: In warfarin-associated ICH, give vitamin K 10 mg IV plus 4F-PCC dosed by INR/weight to rapidly reverse anticoagulation; FFP is an inferior alternative.
caveats: Vitamin K is required for sustained reversal; PCC alone is short-acting.
FFP carries volume-overload risk and slower correction.

guidelines.statement: In patients presenting 4.5-9 h from LKW or with wake-up stroke, IV thrombolysis (alteplase or tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg) selected by perfusion or DWI-FLAIR mismatch is reasonable when EVT is not indicated or available.
caveats: Imaging-based selection is required; unselected late-window thrombolysis is not supported (TWIST).
Time from LKW remains a key safety determinant; TIMELESS extended TNK to 24 h with mismatch selection but the overall primary endpoint did not reach significance.

guidelines.statement: In patients with anterior-circulation LVO and ASPECTS 3-5 (or core 50-100 mL) within 24 h of LKW, EVT is recommended to improve functional outcome.
caveats: Pre-stroke mRS, life expectancy, and goals of care still inform shared decision-making.
ASPECTS <3 was not adequately enrolled in the seminal RCTs and is a Class IIb area.

guidelines.statement: In patients with anterior-circulation LVO presenting 6-24 h from LKW, EVT is recommended for those meeting DAWN or DEFUSE-3 imaging-selection criteria.
caveats: Outside trial-selection criteria, EVT is reasonable on a case-by-case basis with shared decision-making.

guidelines.statement: Tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg (max 25 mg) is a reasonable alternative to alteplase 0.9 mg/kg for IV thrombolysis in eligible AIS within 4.5 h, particularly for patients with anticipated EVT.
caveats: Use TNK 0.25 mg/kg only; the 0.4 mg/kg dose was not non-inferior in EXTEND-IA TNK part 2.
Local protocols may continue to favor alteplase; both are acceptable.

trials.name: DIRECT ANGIO
trials.fullName: Direct versus Conventional Transfer to Angiography Suite in Patients with Severe Acute Stroke Treated with Thrombectomy (France)
trials.teachingPoint: Bypassing pre-EVT imaging increased sICH without benefit and was halted early — cautions against direct-to-angio in suspected LVO.

trials.name: AVERROES
trials.fullName: Apixaban in Patients with Atrial Fibrillation Unsuitable for VKA
trials.teachingPoint: Supports anticoagulation over aspirin for AF stroke prevention generally; the ICH-survivor question is answered by the dedicated trials below.

trials.name: ARTESiA
trials.fullName: Apixaban for Stroke Prevention in Subclinical Atrial Fibrillation
trials.population: n=4012, ≥55, NIHSS n/a, subclinical AF detected on device
trials.teachingPoint: Establishes apixaban benefit in subclinical AF; the ICH-survivor question is addressed by PRESTIGE-AF, SoSTART, APACHE-AF and the COCROACH meta-analysis, which enrolled that population.

trials.name: PRESTIGE-AF
trials.fullName: Prevention of Stroke in Intracerebral Haemorrhage Survivors with Atrial Fibrillation
trials.population: n=319, median 79, NIHSS n/a, Spontaneous ICH survivors with AF, mRS ≤4
trials.finding: Ischaemic stroke markedly reduced (0.83 vs 8.60 per 100 patient-years); recurrent ICH did NOT meet non-inferiority (5.00 vs 0.82 per 100 patient-years)
trials.teachingPoint: Objective: anticoagulation after ICH in AF trades a large ischaemic-stroke reduction against a substantial recurrent-ICH increase; individualize.

trials.name: ENRICH-AF
trials.fullName: Edoxaban for Intracranial Hemorrhage Survivors with Atrial Fibrillation
trials.population: n=948, ≥45, NIHSS n/a, Intracranial-haemorrhage survivors with high-risk AF (CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2)
trials.finding: Full efficacy not yet reported; the lobar-ICH subgroup was stopped by the DSMB for excess recurrent haemorrhagic stroke
trials.teachingPoint: Objective: interim DSMB stopped edoxaban in lobar ICH for excess rebleeding; the main efficacy comparison is awaited.

trials.name: SoSTART
trials.fullName: Start or Stop Anticoagulants Randomised Trial
trials.population: n=203, adults, NIHSS n/a, Survivors of spontaneous intracranial haemorrhage with AF (CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2)
trials.finding: Inconclusive: 8/101 (8%) start vs 4/102 (4%) avoid; starting NOT shown non-inferior
trials.teachingPoint: Objective: did not establish non-inferiority of starting anticoagulation after ICH; hypothesis-generating.

trials.name: APACHE-AF
trials.fullName: Apixaban After Anticoagulation-Associated Intracerebral Haemorrhage in Atrial Fibrillation
trials.population: n=101, median 78, NIHSS n/a, 7-90 d after anticoagulation-associated ICH; AF, CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2, mRS ≤4
trials.finding: No difference: 26% (12.6%/y) apixaban vs 24% (11.9%/y) avoid; high absolute risk in both arms
trials.teachingPoint: Objective: no clear difference; both strategies carry high residual vascular risk.

trials.name: COCROACH
trials.fullName: Oral Anticoagulation after Intracranial Haemorrhage in AF: Individual-Patient-Data Meta-analysis
trials.population: n=412, 75% ≥75, NIHSS n/a, AF with prior spontaneous intracranial haemorrhage (pooled RCTs: SoSTART, APACHE-AF, NASPAF-ICH)
trials.teachingPoint: Objective synthesis: net benefit of anticoagulation after ICH in AF remains statistically uncertain; ischaemic reduction is offset by a non-significant bleeding increase.

trials.name: ELAN
trials.fullName: Early versus Later Anticoagulation for Stroke with Atrial Fibrillation
trials.teachingPoint: Supports early DOAC initiation per stroke severity.

trials.name: TIMING
trials.fullName: Timing of Oral Anticoagulant Therapy in AIS with AF
trials.teachingPoint: Supports earlier DOAC initiation.

trials.name: OPTIMAS
trials.fullName: Optimal Timing of Anticoagulation after Acute Ischaemic Stroke with Atrial Fibrillation
trials.population: n=3621, ≥18, Early ≤4 d vs delayed 7-14 d
trials.teachingPoint: Early DOAC (≤4 d) non-inferior to delayed — do not routinely delay anticoagulation.

trials.name: CATALYST
trials.fullName: IPD Meta-analysis of Anticoagulation Timing after Ischaemic Stroke with AF
trials.population: n=5441, Early ≤4 d vs later ≥5 d
trials.teachingPoint: Pooled IPD shows early DOAC (≤4 d) reduces 30-d composite — supports early initiation.

trials.name: STABLED
trials.fullName: Catheter Ablation and Oral Anticoagulation for Secondary Stroke Prevention in Atrial Fibrillation
trials.population: n=249, 20-85 (mean 71.7), Ablation 1-6 mo after index stroke
trials.teachingPoint: Adding ablation to anticoagulation did not reduce recurrent events in post-stroke AF (underpowered) — does not support routine ablation for secondary prevention.

trials.name: ENCHANTED2/MT
trials.fullName: Intensive BP Control after Endovascular Thrombectomy
trials.teachingPoint: Avoid intensive BP lowering after successful EVT; target 140-180 mmHg.

trials.name: OPTIMAL-BP
trials.fullName: Intensive vs Conventional Blood Pressure Lowering after Successful Endovascular Thrombectomy
trials.population: n=306, ≥18, NIHSS all, After successful EVT (mTICI ≥2b)
trials.teachingPoint: Objective: intensive SBP <140 after successful EVT reduced functional independence; reinforces ENCHANTED2/MT.

trials.name: CREST
trials.fullName: Stenting versus Endarterectomy for Carotid-Artery Stenosis
trials.teachingPoint: CAS and CEA equivalent on the composite; stroke risk favors CEA, MI risk favors CAS.

trials.name: THEIA
trials.fullName: Thrombolysis for Central Retinal Artery Occlusion
trials.population: n=70, ≥18, NIHSS n/a, ≤4.5 h from severe vision loss
trials.teachingPoint: CRAO IVT is reasonable in selected centers; no strong RCT support yet.

trials.name: CHANCE
trials.fullName: Clopidogrel with Aspirin in Acute Minor Stroke or TIA
trials.population: n=5170, ≥40, NIHSS NIHSS ≤3 (or high-risk TIA), ≤24 h
trials.teachingPoint: Founding evidence for short-course DAPT in minor stroke / high-risk TIA.

trials.name: POINT
trials.fullName: Clopidogrel and Aspirin in Acute Ischemic Stroke and High-Risk TIA
trials.teachingPoint: Reinforces 21-day cap on DAPT for minor stroke.

trials.name: THALES
trials.fullName: Ticagrelor and Aspirin or Aspirin Alone in AIS or TIA
trials.teachingPoint: Supports ticagrelor-based DAPT, especially when CYP2C19 LOF suspected.

trials.name: INSPIRES
trials.fullName: Dual Antiplatelet Treatment up to 72 Hours after Ischemic Stroke
trials.population: n=6100, ≥35, NIHSS mild stroke or high-risk TIA, ≤72 h
trials.teachingPoint: Allows DAPT initiation up to 72 h in eligible mild stroke.

trials.name: CHANCE-2
trials.fullName: Ticagrelor or Clopidogrel with Aspirin in High-Risk TIA or Minor Stroke
trials.teachingPoint: Supports ticagrelor over clopidogrel in CYP2C19 LOF carriers.

trials.name: TAPIS
trials.fullName: Ticagrelor with Aspirin Dual Antiplatelet Therapy combined with Intravenous Thrombolysis in Patients with Ischaemic Stroke in China
trials.population: n=1382, Median 65.6, NIHSS 4-10, ≤6 h of onset
trials.teachingPoint: Early oral DAPT within 6 h as an adjunct to thrombolysis improved excellent outcomes in moderate stroke — a strategy long considered contraindicated; small sICH risk not excluded.

trials.name: NAVIGATE ESUS
trials.fullName: Rivaroxaban for Stroke Prevention after Embolic Stroke of Undetermined Source
trials.teachingPoint: Rivaroxaban not superior to aspirin in ESUS and more bleeding — empiric anticoagulation not indicated.

trials.name: RE-SPECT ESUS
trials.fullName: Dabigatran for Prevention of Stroke after Embolic Stroke of Undetermined Source
trials.teachingPoint: Dabigatran not superior to aspirin in ESUS — confirms NAVIGATE; no empiric DOAC.

trials.name: ARCADIA
trials.fullName: Apixaban to Prevent Recurrence after Cryptogenic Stroke in Patients with Atrial Cardiopathy
trials.teachingPoint: Apixaban no better than aspirin in atrial-cardiopathy ESUS — atrial cardiopathy alone does not justify anticoagulation.

trials.name: ATTENTION
trials.fullName: Endovascular Treatment of Acute Basilar-Artery Occlusion
trials.teachingPoint: Established EVT benefit for basilar-artery occlusion within 12 h.

trials.name: BAOCHE
trials.fullName: Thrombectomy 6 to 24 Hours after Stroke Due to Basilar-Artery Occlusion
trials.teachingPoint: Extends EVT benefit for basilar-artery occlusion to 6-24 h.

trials.name: SELECT2
trials.fullName: Trial of Endovascular Thrombectomy for Large Ischemic Strokes
trials.teachingPoint: Supports EVT for large-core; large core is no longer an automatic exclusion.

trials.name: ANGEL-ASPECT
trials.fullName: Endovascular Therapy for Acute Ischemic Stroke with Large Infarct
trials.teachingPoint: Confirms EVT benefit in large-core population.

trials.name: RESCUE-Japan LIMIT
trials.fullName: Endovascular Therapy for Acute Stroke with Large Ischemic Region
trials.teachingPoint: First major RCT to support large-core EVT.

trials.name: TENSION
trials.fullName: Endovascular Thrombectomy for AIS with Established Large Infarct
trials.teachingPoint: European replication of large-core EVT benefit.

trials.name: LASTE
trials.fullName: Thrombectomy for Stroke with a Large Infarct of Unrestricted Size
trials.teachingPoint: Thrombectomy benefits even very large infarcts (ASPECTS ≤5), at the cost of more sICH.

trials.name: ATLAS
trials.fullName: Endovascular Thrombectomy for Large-Core Ischaemic Stroke up to 24 h — Systematic Review and IPD Meta-analysis with Central Imaging Adjudication
trials.teachingPoint: Consolidates EVT benefit across large-core strata to 24 h, except very extensive cores (≥150 mL) beyond 6 h where evidence remains limited.

trials.name: DAWN
trials.fullName: Thrombectomy 6 to 24 Hours after Stroke with Mismatch between Deficit and Infarct
trials.teachingPoint: Foundational evidence for late-window EVT.

trials.name: DEFUSE-3
trials.fullName: Thrombectomy at 6-16 Hours with Selection by Perfusion Imaging
trials.teachingPoint: Established perfusion-mismatch criteria for late-window EVT.

trials.name: DISTAL
trials.fullName: Endovascular Treatment for Medium or Distal Vessel Occlusion Stroke — 12-Month Outcomes
trials.population: n=543, Median 77, NIHSS Median 6, ≤6 h, or 6-24 h with salvageable tissue
trials.teachingPoint: No long-term benefit of EVT in mild-to-moderate medium/distal vessel occlusion — routine thrombectomy not supported in this population.

trials.name: ESCAPE-MeVO
trials.fullName: Endovascular Treatment for Medium Vessel Occlusion Stroke
trials.population: n=530, Mean 69.8, NIHSS Median 8, ≤24 h (median 4.6 h)
trials.teachingPoint: Does not support routine endovascular thrombectomy for isolated medium vessel occlusion stroke outside of clinical trials.

trials.name: EXTEND
trials.fullName: Thrombolysis Guided by Perfusion Imaging up to 9 Hours after Onset
trials.teachingPoint: Supports perfusion-mismatch IVT in 4.5-9 h window when EVT not indicated.

trials.name: EPITHET
trials.fullName: Echoplanar Imaging Thrombolytic Evaluation Trial
trials.finding: Reduced infarct growth in mismatch group; clinical co-primary not significant
trials.teachingPoint: Conceptual basis for later mismatch-based late-window trials.

trials.name: ECASS4-EXTEND
trials.fullName: Extending the Time Window for IV Thrombolysis Using MRI-based Selection
trials.finding: Favored alteplase numerically; not statistically significant after early termination
trials.teachingPoint: Adds to the late-window mismatch evidence base; not independently practice-changing.

trials.name: TIMELESS
trials.fullName: Tenecteplase for Stroke at 4.5 to 24 Hours with Perfusion-Imaging Selection
trials.finding: Favored TNK numerically; primary endpoint did not reach significance overall
trials.teachingPoint: Cautionary: late-window TNK alone (with EVT permitted) did not meet its primary endpoint.

trials.name: TRACE-III
trials.fullName: Tenecteplase for Ischemic Stroke 4.5-24 Hours without Thrombectomy
trials.teachingPoint: Strongest evidence to date for late-window TNK in EVT-ineligible LVO.

trials.name: HOPE
trials.fullName: Alteplase for Acute Ischemic Stroke at 4.5 to 24 Hours: The HOPE Randomized Clinical Trial
trials.teachingPoint: Adds positive evidence for perfusion-selected late-window IV thrombolysis in EVT-ineligible patients; remains selective and consult-dependent, not routine.

trials.name: EXPECTS
trials.fullName: Alteplase for Posterior Circulation Ischemic Stroke at 4.5 to 24 Hours
trials.population: n=234, adults, NIHSS median 3 (mainly mild), 4.5-24 h
trials.teachingPoint: Supports late-window thrombolysis for selected mild posterior-circulation stroke without perfusion mismatch requirement; remains emerging, not routine.

trials.name: CHABLIS-T II
trials.fullName: Tenecteplase Thrombolysis for Stroke up to 24 Hours After Onset With Perfusion Imaging Selection (CHABLIS-T II)
trials.finding: Improved reperfusion without a significant clinical-outcome difference
trials.teachingPoint: Adds to the mixed late-window TNK evidence base; reinforces that reperfusion gains have not consistently translated to clinical benefit.

trials.name: CHOICE
trials.fullName: Intra-arterial Alteplase Following Successful Thrombectomy
trials.teachingPoint: IA alteplase after successful EVT may improve outcomes; not yet standard of care.

trials.name: CHOICE-2
trials.fullName: Adjunctive Intra-Arterial Alteplase After Successful Thrombectomy for Acute Ischemic Stroke
trials.teachingPoint: IA alteplase after successful EVT improved excellent outcomes and reperfusion — but a higher mortality signal warrants caution before adoption.

trials.name: ANNEXA-I
trials.fullName: Andexanet for Factor Xa Inhibitor-Associated Acute ICH
trials.population: n=530, ≥18, NIHSS n/a, ≤15 h from last FXa dose, ≤6 h from ICH onset
trials.teachingPoint: AHA/ASA 2022 lists andexanet as Class IIa for FXa-associated ICH; ANNEXA-I refines hemostatic-vs-thrombotic trade-off.

trials.name: INTERACT3
trials.fullName: Care Bundle Approach for Acute Intracerebral Haemorrhage
trials.population: n=7036, ≥18, NIHSS n/a, <6 h from onset
trials.teachingPoint: Establishes care-bundle approach for acute ICH.

trials.name: INTERACT2
trials.fullName: Rapid Blood-Pressure Lowering in Acute Intracerebral Hemorrhage
trials.teachingPoint: Supports early BP reduction in ICH; ordinal benefit despite a negative dichotomous primary.

trials.name: ATACH-2
trials.fullName: Intensive Blood-Pressure Lowering in Acute Cerebral Hemorrhage
trials.teachingPoint: More intensive target (<140) gave no benefit and more renal AEs — avoid overshoot below ~140.

trials.name: TRIDENT
trials.fullName: Three Low-Dose Antihypertensive Agents in a Single Pill after Intracerebral Hemorrhage
trials.teachingPoint: A simplified low-dose triple pill after ICH lowers recurrent stroke and CV events — supports intensive, simplified BP-lowering.

trials.name: ENRICH
trials.fullName: Early Minimally Invasive Removal of Intracerebral Hemorrhage
trials.population: n=300, 18-80, NIHSS n/a, ≤24 h from onset
trials.teachingPoint: Supports early minimally invasive evacuation for lobar ICH at capable centers.

trials.name: MISTIE III
trials.fullName: Minimally Invasive Surgery with Thrombolysis in ICH Evacuation
trials.teachingPoint: No overall functional benefit; safe; outcome depends on achieving adequate evacuation.

trials.name: MIND
trials.fullName: Minimally Invasive Surgery vs Medical Management Alone for Intracerebral Hemorrhage: The MIND Randomized Clinical Trial
trials.teachingPoint: Tempers enthusiasm for minimally invasive ICH evacuation; MIS benefit appears pathway- and technique-specific (cf. ENRICH), not universal.

trials.name: SWITCH
trials.fullName: Decompressive Craniectomy plus Best Medical Treatment vs Best Medical Treatment Alone for Severe Deep ICH
trials.population: n=201, 18-75, NIHSS n/a, Severe deep (basal ganglia/thalamic) spontaneous ICH
trials.finding: Favoured surgery numerically but not significant: 44% vs 58%
trials.teachingPoint: Objective: numerically fewer very-poor outcomes with craniectomy, but not statistically significant; no safety excess.

trials.name: Hyponatremia & ICH
trials.fullName: Severity-Stratified Hyponatremia Is Associated with Increased Mortality and Complications in Nontraumatic Intracerebral Hemorrhage
trials.population: n=45114, Serum Na ≤7 d of diagnosis
trials.teachingPoint: Serum sodium is a readily-available risk-stratification marker in acute ICH; supports vigilant Na monitoring (causality not established).

trials.name: RESPECT
trials.fullName: Long-Term Outcomes of Patent Foramen Ovale Closure or Medical Therapy after Stroke
trials.teachingPoint: Long-term PFO closure reduces recurrent stroke vs medical therapy in selected patients <60 y.

trials.name: CLOSE
trials.fullName: Patent Foramen Ovale Closure or Anticoagulation vs Antiplatelets after Stroke
trials.teachingPoint: Closure markedly cuts recurrent stroke in high-risk PFO anatomy (ASA / large shunt).

trials.name: REDUCE
trials.fullName: Patent Foramen Ovale Closure or Antiplatelet Therapy for Cryptogenic Stroke
trials.teachingPoint: Closure reduces recurrent stroke and new infarcts; watch for AF and device events.

trials.name: OCEANIC-STROKE
trials.fullName: Asundexian for Secondary Stroke Prevention
trials.teachingPoint: Asundexian on top of antiplatelets cut ischaemic stroke ~26% without more major bleeding — could reshape noncardioembolic prevention.

trials.name: Suicide after stroke
trials.fullName: Increased Risk of Suicide after Stroke: A Population-Based Matched Cohort Study
trials.finding: Higher in stroke survivors: 11.1 vs 3.2 per 10,000 person-years
trials.teachingPoint: Supports long-term (beyond 1 year) suicidality screening in survivors — especially younger and lower-income patients.

trials.name: AcT
trials.fullName: Intravenous Tenecteplase Compared with Alteplase for Acute Ischaemic Stroke in Canada
trials.teachingPoint: Supports TNK 0.25 mg/kg as a preferred IV thrombolytic for IVT-eligible AIS within 4.5 h.

trials.name: TRACE-2
trials.fullName: Trial of Tenecteplase in Chinese Patients with Acute Ischemic Stroke
trials.teachingPoint: Confirms non-inferiority of TNK in non-LVO eligible AIS within 4.5 h.

trials.name: ORIGINAL
trials.fullName: Tenecteplase vs Alteplase in AIS (ORIGINAL)
trials.teachingPoint: Adds further RCT support for TNK as alteplase-equivalent first-line.

trials.name: TNK vs alteplase · RWE
trials.fullName: Real-World Efficacy and Safety of Tenecteplase versus Alteplase in Acute Ischemic Stroke: A Propensity Score-Matched Analysis
trials.teachingPoint: Adds real-world support for tenecteplase as a practical alteplase alternative — consistent with trial-level non-inferiority.

trials.name: BRIDGE-TNK
trials.fullName: Intravenous Tenecteplase before Thrombectomy in Stroke
trials.teachingPoint: Reinforces bridging IV thrombolysis before thrombectomy for eligible LVO within 4.5 h; do not routinely skip IVT in eligible patients headed to EVT.

trials.name: TEMPO-2
trials.fullName: Tenecteplase versus Standard of Care for Minor Ischaemic Stroke with Proven Occlusion (TEMPO-2)
trials.finding: No benefit of tenecteplase vs standard of care
trials.teachingPoint: Argues against routine thrombolysis for minor non-disabling stroke solely because an occlusion is present; keep the disabling-deficit gate.

trials.name: WAKE-UP
trials.fullName: MRI-Guided Thrombolysis for Stroke with Unknown Time of Onset
trials.population: n=503, 18-80, NIHSS NIHSS ≤25, unknown onset (last seen well >4.5 h)
trials.teachingPoint: Foundational evidence for imaging-selected thrombolysis in wake-up / unknown onset stroke.

trials.name: TWIST
trials.fullName: Tenecteplase in Wake-up Ischaemic Stroke Trial
trials.teachingPoint: Late-window thrombolysis without advanced imaging is not supported.

education.title: AFib Anticoagulation Restart Timing
education.summary: Evidence-based schedule for starting or restarting DOACs after acute ischemic stroke or TIA.

references.\"citation\": Fischer U et al. N Engl J Med. 2023;388:2411-2421.
Dehbi HM et al. Lancet 2025.
Joglar JA et al. 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline. Circulation. 2024;149:e1-e156.

education.body:
Evidence-based schedule for starting or restarting DOACs after acute ischemic stroke or TIA.

education.title: Antiepileptic Drugs & Post-Stroke Seizures
education.summary: Clinical classification of post-stroke seizures, guideline-directed management, comparison of first-line and second-line antiepileptic drugs (ASMs), and post-stroke epilepsy risk scores (SeLECT and IsCHEMiA).

references.\"citation\": Prabhakaran S, et al. 2026 Guideline for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke. Stroke. 2026.
Greenberg SM, et al. 2022 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Spontaneous Intracerebral Hemorrhage. Stroke. 2022;53(7):e282-e361.
Hoh BL, et al. 2023 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Aneurysmal Subarachnoid Hemorrhage. Stroke. 2023;54(7):e314-e370.
IsCHEMiA in Vascular Epilepsy: Identifying Risks for Post Stroke Epilepsy. Epilepsy Currents. 2026;26.
Galovic M, et al. SeLECT: a prediction model for late seizures after ischemic stroke. Lancet Neurol. 2018;17(2):143-152.

education.body:
Clinical classification of post-stroke seizures, guideline-directed management, comparison of first-line and second-line antiepileptic drugs (ASMs), and post-stroke epilepsy risk scores (SeLECT and IsCHEMiA).

education.title: Aspirin Failure & Resistance
education.summary: Clinical definition, mechanisms of resistance, diagnostic evaluation, and evidence-based secondary prevention strategies for patients who stroke on aspirin.

references.\"citation\": Kleindorfer DO, et al. 2021 Stroke Prevention. Stroke. 2021;52:e364-e467.
Failure of Antithrombotic Therapy and Risk of Stroke in Patients With Symptomatic Intracranial Stenosis. Stroke. 2009;40:359-364.
CAPRIE Steering Committee. Lancet. 1996;348:1329-1339.
Wang Y, et al. Clopidogrel with Aspirin in Acute Minor Stroke or Transient Ischemic Attack. N Engl J Med. 2013;369:11-19.
Johnston SC, et al. Clopidogrel and Aspirin in Acute Ischemic Stroke and High-Risk TIA. N Engl J Med. 2018;379:215-225.

education.body:
